Well, good morning ministers. Welcome, um, to the Education Workforce Select Committee. Welcome back, Tim. Uh, we've got half an hour with you this morning. Uh, as you know, uh, minister and I have collected responsibility for this area. So I'll speak quickly first, and then I'll hand over to Minister ti. So, uh, I just like to highlight a few of the things. Uh, from my perspective, obviously, the disestablishment of N-Z-I-S-T has been a major milestone, and I would really like to acknowledge the strong support that I've had from TC and the ministry, uh, during that.

Just to give that continuity and assurance that on one January there will be staff there. Uh, I'd like to acknowledge the incredible work that has been done by the polytechnics over the last 18 months, improving their financial performance, the 10 standalone polytechnics particularly, but also those that are still left in, uh, NZID.

They have also made really good progress, progress with their financial situation. And, uh, we hope to be looking at them quite early in the new year. So we're doing a lot of work on that now. But, uh, substantively, that's all I wanted to say. So, uh, turn the time back to the committee and thank you. Thank You, Shannon Halbert. Thank you. Um, uh, minister Simmons, I'll, I'll ask questions of you first. So we're in an economic downturn. Your next numbers have hit 90,000, in your words, 10,500 more in the last 12 months.

How does it make sense to cut the apprenticeship boost scheme, which means thousands of less apprenticeships, apprentices are enrolling and graduating when in fact our workforce tells us that we need more in the next few years? Hmm. Um, thank you. Yes, you're quite right.

The needs numbers are, are higher and we know that in times of high unemployment youth are usually impacted, uh, more significantly. Um, a BI apprentice boost, of course, was a COVID initiated, um, uh, uh, solution to the issues of COVID. It always was finishing the funding for that finished at the end of 2024.

So in fact, we picked up and, uh, continued some funding for it, but not the full amount that had been there during COVID. Uh, I guess the, the issue around a BI is, it is not a full subsidy of, of, uh, employing an apprentice. It's a small amount, uh, to encourage those that, uh, are taking on apprentices to support them through.

So if the work is not there, the apprentices won't get taken on whether there's an, um, apprentice boost or not. So, TUC has confirmed that yes, demand has dropped away from employers. Mm-hmm. But why would the government remove the one incentive that supports employers to pick up an apprentice?

Well, because the amount is never going to pay for the full, uh, cost of the apprentice. And if the work isn't there, you can't take an apprentice on. So as the economic recovery occurs, as construction picks up as we are seeing the consents are picking up, as the infrastructure, um, builds take place, then there will be more apprentices taken on.

And this is a, a small incentive, uh, that assists to support the apprentices when, uh, there, but it's never going to be enough to make an employer employ an apprentice if there isn't work there. So do you not see the correlation between the drop of apprenticeships by the thousands under your watch and your removal of funding that supports people to take up learning?

No, absolutely not. I see the drop in apprenticeships being the economic recovery, having to take place from the very poor fiscal discipline that was shown by the previous government. And this government having to work our way out of an economic, uh, situation that wasn't of our making.

That meant that we had to get inflation under control. We had to get interest rates back so that businesses had the confidence to be able to employ again. So that is the reason why apprentices weren't being taken on. As we pull outta this, um, economic situation, then apprentices will come back as the, the building, uh, in construction increases, and we're seeing it happening in the south island and in the rural areas particularly, we are seeing consents increase.

We are seeing the infrastructure work, the six or 7 billion of infrastructure work that is going to start, uh, very soon, that's when more apprentices will come on. But the apprentice boost is always only a small amount to support, uh, those employers when they do have apprentices. Minister, you have 90,000 young people in this country not engaged in education, employment, or training. Correct.

What Are you doing about that? Well, we're ensuring that we have got the provision of the, uh, sort of, uh, programs and vocational education that will give them a pathway to employment. The sad situation is that when an economy has been mismanaged, that there is a hard reality of getting back to the basics of getting inflation under control, of getting interest rates under control, of getting businesses in a situation where they can employ again.

And that is the reality of an unemployment rising. As we gain control of the economy, again, that means that, uh, we are not going to see those young people going into apprenticeships that we would like. But as the, as the economy comes out of that recession, we will see, um, the employment pick up and we'll see apprenticeships pick Up. Your Prime Minister has been very clear that young people need to get out of their house off PlayStation, go and get a job wherever that is, or go into training.

There's less opportunities for those living in regional communities. There's less apprenticeships available and there's less foundation learning available because you won't fund it.

My question is, what are you doing to address the 90,000 needs numbers that you have raised under your watch? Well, certainly we have put in place things like, uh, a greater number of, um, youth guarantee places 175 extra youth guarantee places.

Uh, the, the programs, the courses that have been closed by the Polytechnic are not full of people. Polytechs don't close courses that have 18 or 20 or even 16 people in them. They close courses that aren't viable because they've got very small numbers in them. We are ensuring that we have in place a network of polytechnics assisted by a federation, which will give all those polytechnics with quite, uh, sparse populations over large geographic regions, access to, uh, distance delivery via the open polytechnic.

So the portfolio of programs that will be available to a number of those young people out in those, um, disperse, um, geographic regions such as the far north will have a, a much greater, much greater portfolio of programs they will have access to.

So we haven't taken away where there's demand, where there is strong demand and numbers are high, those programs are viable when there, where there are smaller numbers, and we still need to have a pathway to employment. We're ensuring through access of online delivery in conjunction with that federation, so on campus and online, blended delivery will be available for those, um, those young people that are in many cases in quite isolated communities. Okay.

One final step, uh, just quickly is, so in your forecast in the year ahead, are your needs numbers going up while are they going down? Uh, I believe that we've hit about peak unemployment and we expect it to go down. The needs may take a little longer than the general, um, unemployment to go down.
